title: Birdies, bogeys, eagles — the scoring language
microFact: Birdie = 1 under par on a hole. Bogey = 1 over. Learn these and half of golf conversation unlocks.
lesson:
Golf scores on a hole are named relative to par:

• EAGLE: 2 under par (a 3 on a par 5)
• BIRDIE: 1 under par
• PAR: even
• BOGEY: 1 over
• DOUBLE BOGEY: 2 over — then triple, quadruple...
• ALBATROSS (or double eagle): 3 under — rarer than a hole-in-one
• ACE: a hole-in-one

The bird theme started with 19th-century American slang where "bird" meant something excellent.

Conversation fluency matters: when a playing partner says "I was two over through twelve," they mean 2 over par after 12 holes. When someone asks "what did you make?" walking off a green, answer in this language: "double" beats "I got a six." Small thing, instant insider signal.

Tradition alert: if you ever make an ace, you buy drinks for everyone at the clubhouse. Non-negotiable.

quiz.question: You score a 4 on a par 5. What's it called?
quiz.explanation: One under par on the hole = birdie, regardless of which hole or par it is.
